1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	
6	\section{Setup and motivation}
7	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
8	
9	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
10	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
11	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
12	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
13	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
14	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
15	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
16	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
17	single non-zero element of the base ring.
18	
19	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
20	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
21	\(\F\).
22	
23	\section{Generic flatness}
24	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
25	
26	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
27	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
28	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
29	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
30	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
31	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
32	non-zero element of the base ring.
33	
34	\begin{theorem}
35	
36	
37	
38	\leanok
39	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
40	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
41	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
42	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
43	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
44	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
45	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
48	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
49	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
50	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
51	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
52	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
53	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
54	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
55	
56	\end{theorem}
57	
58	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
59	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
60	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
61	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
62	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
63	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
64	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
65	% there exists a finite filtration
66	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
67	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
68	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
69	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
70	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
71	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
72	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
73	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
74	\begin{proof}
75	
76	  \textbf{Primary route — the finite \(A\)-module case.}
77	  Suppose first that \(M\) is module-finite over \(A\) itself. Since \(A\) is noetherian,
78	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). The generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\)
79	  (where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\)) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free
80	  over \(K\); by \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} this freeness descends to a single basic open:
81	  there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\). This closes the
82	  theorem whenever \(M\) is finite over \(A\).
83	
84	  \textbf{Surviving residue — reducing the finite-type \(B\)-algebra case to the
85	  finite \(A\)-module case.} The genuine content not supplied by Mathlib is the
86	  reduction of the general hypothesis (\(M\) finite over a \emph{finite-type}
87	  \(A\)-algebra \(B\)) to the finite \(A\)-module situation handled above. Concretely,
88	  the missing piece is the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness: a finite module
89	  over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) becomes free over \(A_f\) after inverting a single
90	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). The classical \S4 dévissage below is the fallback route
91	  for exactly this residue.
92	
93	  \emph{Fallback (Nitsure \S4 induction).}
94	  The \S4 dévissage is assembled from the sub-lemmas of
95	  \cref{sec:gf_devissage_chain}. If \(M_K = 0\), the torsion base case
96	  \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} gives \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f = 0\) free. Otherwise the
97	  prime-filtration induction principle \cref{lem:noeth_prime_filtration} over the
98	  noetherian ring \(B\), combined with the splicing fact
99	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}, reduces to \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) and hence to
100	  \(B\) a domain, \(M = B\); Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators
101	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators} makes \(B_g\) finite over a polynomial
102	  ring \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the polynomial-ring core
103	  \cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core} — bottoming out at the finite-\(A\)-module leaf
104	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} of the primary route — produces the required
105	  \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\).
106	\end{proof}
107	
108	\subsection{Dévissage chain for the finite-type residue}
109	\label{sec:gf_devissage_chain}
110	
111	The following sub-lemmas decompose the surviving residue of
112	\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} — the case where \(M\) is finite over the
113	finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\) but not module-finite over \(A\) — into the
114	classical \cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 induction. They are ordered so that each block
115	depends only on earlier blocks or Mathlib lemmas. Throughout,
116	\(A\) is a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), \(B\) a
117	finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module with the compatible
118	\(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\).
119	
120	\begin{lemma}\leanok
121	[Finite-module leaf — finite \(A\)-module case]
122	  \label{lem:gf_finite_module}
123	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite}
124	  \uses{lem:fp_free_descent}
125	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there
126	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
127	\end{lemma}
128	
129	\begin{proof}
130	
131	  This is the \(d = 0\) leaf of the induction. A finite module over a noetherian ring is
132	  finitely presented, so
133	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). Over the generic point
134	  \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A) = S^{-1}A\) (\(S = A \setminus \{0\}\)) the localisation
135	  \(M_K = S^{-1}M\) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free over
136	  \(K\). The finite-presentation free-descent lemma \cref{lem:fp_free_descent}
137	  then descends this freeness to a single basic open: there is \(r \in S\), i.e.\
138	  \(r \in A\) with \(r \ne 0\), such that \(M_r\) is free over \(A_r\). Take
139	  \(f := r\).
140	\end{proof}
141	
142	\begin{lemma}[Finite-presentation free descent]
143	  \label{lem:fp_free_descent}
144	  \lean{Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers}
145	  \mathlibok
146	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
147	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative subset, and
148	  \(M\) a finitely-presented \(R\)-module such that the localisation \(M_S\) is
149	  free over the localisation \(R_S\). Then there exists \(r \in S\) such that
150	  \(M_r\) is free over \(R_r\) (of the same finite rank).
151	\end{lemma}
152	
153	\begin{lemma}\leanok
154	[L1 — torsion base case \(M_K = 0\)]
155	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_base}
156	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_torsion}
157	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case
158	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1726).
159	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
160	  % "We argue by induction on $n$, starting with $n=-1$ which is the case when
161	  %  $M_K =0$. In this case, as $K\otimes _AM = S^{-1}M$ where $S = A - \{ 0\}$,
162	  %  each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
163	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating elements,
164	  %  we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$. Hence $M_f =0$, proving
165	  %  the lemma when $n=-1$."
166	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (base case of the
167	  induction).}
168	  Suppose \(K \otimes_A M = 0\), where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Then there exists
169	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(f M = 0\); consequently \(M_f = 0\) is free over
170	  \(A_f\).
171	\end{lemma}
172	
173	\begin{proof}
174	
175	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1719-L1726).
176	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (base case).}
177	  Since \(K \otimes_A M = S^{-1}M\) with \(S = A \setminus \{0\}\), the hypothesis
178	  \(M_K = 0\) means every element of \(M\) is killed by some non-zero element of
179	  \(A\). Choose a finite \(B\)-generating set \(v_1, \dots, v_k\) of \(M\) (which
180	  exists because \(M\) is a finite \(B\)-module), and for each \(i\) an
181	  \(a_i \in A\), \(a_i \ne 0\), with \(a_i v_i = 0\). The product
182	  \(f := a_1 \cdots a_k\) is non-zero (\(A\) is a domain) and annihilates every
183	  generator, hence \(f M = 0\). Therefore \(M_f = 0\), which is free over \(A_f\).
184	\end{proof}
185	
186	\begin{lemma}[L2 — prime-filtration induction principle]
187	  \label{lem:noeth_prime_filtration}
188	  \lean{IsNoetherianRing.induction_on_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime}
189	  \mathlibok
190	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
191	  Let \(B\) be a noetherian commutative ring and let \(P\) be a predicate on finite
192	  \(B\)-modules that (i) holds for every subsingleton module, (ii) holds for every
193	  cyclic quotient \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) with \(\mathfrak{p}\) prime, and (iii) is
194	  closed under short exact sequences (\(P(N_1)\) and \(P(N_3)\) imply \(P(N_2)\)
195	  for \(0 \to N_1 \to N_2 \to N_3 \to 0\)). Then \(P\) holds for every finite
196	  \(B\)-module. In particular every finite \(B\)-module \(M\) admits a finite
197	  filtration \(0 = M_0 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M\) with each quotient
198	  \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B/\mathfrak{p}_i\) for a prime \(\mathfrak{p}_i\), so to
199	  prove generic freeness for \(M\) it suffices to prove it for each
200	  \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) and to splice along the filtration's short exact sequences.
201	\end{lemma}
202	
203	\begin{lemma}\leanok
204	[L3a — localisation of a short exact sequence is exact]
205	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}
206	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exact_localizedModule_powers_of_shortExact}
207	  Let \(0 \to M' \xrightarrow{i} M \xrightarrow{q} M'' \to 0\) be a short exact
208	  sequence of \(B\)-modules, and let \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). Restricting scalars
209	  along \(A \to B\) and localising every term at the powers of \(f\) yields a short
210	  exact sequence
211	  \[
212	    0 \to M'_f \xrightarrow{i_f} M_f \xrightarrow{q_f} M''_f \to 0
213	  \]
214	  of \(A_f\)-modules; in particular \(i_f\) is injective and \(q_f\) is surjective.
215	\end{lemma}
216	
217	\begin{proof}
218	
219	  Localisation at a multiplicative set is an exact functor, preserving injections,
220	  surjections, and exactness. Applying it to the scalar-restricted \(A\)-linear maps
221	  \(i\) and \(q\) (via \(A \to B\)) returns the displayed sequence.
222	\end{proof}
223	
224	\begin{lemma}\leanok
225	[L3b — free transport across a finer localisation]
226	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport}
227	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_free_of_eq_mul}
228	  Let \(N\) be a \(B\)-module and \(f', f'' \in A\) with \(f = f' f''\). If the
229	  localisation \(N_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\), then the further localisation
230	  \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
231	\end{lemma}
232	
233	\begin{proof}
234	
235	  Because \(f = f' f''\), the localisation away from \(f\) factors through the
236	  localisation away from \(f'\): the ring \(A_f\) is the localisation of \(A_{f'}\)
237	  at the image of \(f''\), and likewise the module \(N_f\) is the localisation of
238	  \(N_{f'}\) at the image of \(f''\). A localisation of a free module is free over
239	  the localised base ring — a basis of \(N_{f'}\) over \(A_{f'}\) maps to a basis of
240	  \(N_f\) over \(A_f\). Hence \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Stated as a standalone
241	  fact, it is applied to both ends of the spliced sequence.
242	\end{proof}
243	
244	\begin{lemma}\leanok
245	[L3c — a short exact sequence with free ends has a free middle]
246	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
247	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_of_shortExact_of_free_free}
248	  Let \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of
249	  \(A_f\)-modules in which both ends \(M'_f\) and \(M''_f\) are free over \(A_f\).
250	  Then \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\); concretely \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\).
251	\end{lemma}
252	
253	\begin{proof}
254	
255	  A free module is projective, so the free quotient \(M''_f\) is projective and the
256	  short exact sequence splits: \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\). A finite direct sum
257	  of free \(A_f\)-modules is free, hence \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
258	\end{proof}
259	
260	\begin{lemma}\leanok
261	[L3 — splicing fact for a short exact sequence (assembly)]
262	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}
263	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_shortExact}
264	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
265	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
266	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, splicing remark
267	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1737-L1745).
268	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
269	  % "Note that if $0\to M'\to M\to M'' \to 0$ is a short exact sequence of
270	  %  $B$-modules, and if $f'$ and $f''$ are non-zero elements of $A$ such that
271	  %  $M'_{f'}$ and $M''_{f''}$ are free over respectively $A_{f'}$ and $A_{f''}$,
272	  %  then $M_f$ is a free module over $A_f$ where $f=f'f''$. We will use this fact
273	  %  repeatedly. Therefore it is enough to prove the result when $M$ is of the form
274	  %  $B/\pp$ for a prime ideal $\pp$ in $B$. This reduces us to the case where $B$
275	  %  is a domain and $M=B$."
276	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (splicing fact).}
277	  Let \(0 \to M' \to M \to M'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of \(B\)-modules,
278	  and let \(f', f'' \in A\) be non-zero with \(M'_{f'}\) free over \(A_{f'}\) and
279	  \(M''_{f''}\) free over \(A_{f''}\). Then for \(f := f' f''\) the localisation
280	  \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
281	\end{lemma}
282	
283	\begin{proof}
284	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
285	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
286	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1737-L1745).
287	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (splicing fact).}
288	  Set \(f := f' f''\); since \(A\) is a domain and \(f', f'' \ne 0\), the product is
289	  non-zero, so \(f \ne 0\). Localising the given short exact sequence at the powers
290	  of \(f\) (after restricting scalars to \(A\)) produces a short exact sequence
291	  \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) of \(A_f\)-modules, by
292	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}. Both ends are free over \(A_f\):
293	  applying \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport} to \(M'\) with the
294	  factorisation \(f = f' f''\) (using that \(M'_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\)) shows
295	  \(M'_f\) is free over \(A_f\), and applying the same lemma to \(M''\) with the
296	  factorisation \(f = f'' f'\) (using that \(M''_{f''}\) is free over \(A_{f''}\))
297	  shows \(M''_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Finally
298	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split} concludes that the middle term \(M_f\) is
299	  free over \(A_f\).
300	\end{proof}
301	
302	\begin{lemma}[L4 anchor — Noether normalisation over a field]
303	  \label{lem:noether_normalization_fg}
304	  \lean{exists_finite_inj_algHom_of_fg}
305	  \mathlibok
306	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
307	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(R\) a non-trivial finite-type \(k\)-algebra. Then there
308	  exist \(s \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective \(k\)-algebra homomorphism
309	  \(k[X_1, \dots, X_s] \to R\) that is module-finite, i.e.\ \(R\) is a finite module
310	  over a polynomial subalgebra on algebraically independent generators.
311	\end{lemma}
312	
313	\begin{lemma}
314	
315	
316	\leanok
317	[L4a — clearing one equation's denominators]
318	  \label{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
319	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_clear_one_denominator}
320	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
321	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1755-L1759).
322	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
323	  % "If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for
324	  %  coefficients of equations of integral dependence
325	  %  satisfied by a finite set of
326	  %  algebra generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$,
327	  %  we see that $B_g$ is finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
328	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (common denominator).}
329	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), and let
330	  \(p \in K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) be a polynomial with coefficients in \(K\) (for instance,
331	  one coefficient polynomial of an equation of integral dependence over
332	  \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\)). Then there exists \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that \(p\)
333	  lies in the image of the coefficient-extension map
334	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\); equivalently, \(p\) has all of its
335	  coefficients in \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\).
336	\end{lemma}
337	
338	\begin{proof}
339	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1755-L1759).
340	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (common denominator).}
341	  The polynomial \(p\) has finitely many non-zero coefficients
342	  \(c_1, \dots, c_r \in K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Each \(c_j\) is a fraction
343	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) with \(a_j \in A\) and \(s_j \in A \setminus \{0\}\). Put
344	  \(g := s_1 \cdots s_r \in A\); since \(A\) is a domain and each \(s_j \ne 0\), also
345	  \(g \ne 0\). For each \(j\) the denominator \(s_j\) divides \(g\), so
346	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) lies in the subring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\). Hence every
347	  coefficient of \(p\) lies in \(A_g\), and \(p\) is the coefficient-extension of a
348	  polynomial in \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
349	\end{proof}
350	
351	\begin{lemma}\leanok
352	[L4 — Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators]
353	  \label{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}
354	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_localizationAway_finite_mvPolynomial}
355	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator,
356	        lem:gf_isLocalization_lift_injective}
357	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
358	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1747-L1758).
359	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
360	  % "By Noether normalisation lemma, there exist elements $b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B$,
361	  %  such that $K\otimes_AB$ is finite over its subalgebra $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$ and
362	  %  the elements $b_1,\ldots, b_n$ are algebraically independent over $K$. [...]
363	  %  If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for coefficients of
364	  %  equations of integral dependence satisfied by a finite set of algebra
365	  %  generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$, we see that $B_g$ is
366	  %  finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
367	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (Noether
368	  normalisation with denominators).}
369	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K\) and \(B\) a finite-type
370	  \(A\)-algebra that is a domain with \(B_K = K \otimes_A B \ne 0\). Write
371	  \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\) and \(B_g = B[g^{-1}]\) for the localisations away from \(g\)
372	  and its image. Then there exist a natural number \(n\), an element \(g \in A\) with
373	  \(g \ne 0\), and an injective \(A_g\)-algebra homomorphism
374	  \[
375	    \varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \longrightarrow B_g,
376	  \]
377	  where \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is the polynomial ring on \(n\) algebraically
378	  independent variables, such that \(B_g\) is module-finite over the image polynomial
379	  subalgebra via \(\varphi\). The injectivity of \(\varphi\) records the algebraic
380	  independence of the generators \(b_j := \varphi(X_j)\), and module-finiteness is
381	  taken over the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\).
382	\end{lemma}
383	
384	\begin{proof}
385	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
386	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
387	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
388	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
389	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
390	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
391	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
392	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
393	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
394	
395	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
396	  finite set \(\sigma\) of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over
397	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). By Step 1 each generator's image
398	  \(1 \otimes x \in B_K\) is integral over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). For each
399	  \(x \in \sigma\), a standard integrality-and-localization descent argument yields a
400	  non-zero \(a_x \in A\) such that \(a_x \cdot (1 \otimes x)\)
401	  is integral over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Multiplying over the finite
402	  set, set \(g_1 := \prod_{x \in \sigma} a_x\) and \(g := g_0 \cdot g_1\) (with \(g_0\) the
403	  Step-1 denominator witness). After inverting \(g\), every chosen generator becomes
404	  integral over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) and the generators generate \(B_g\) as an algebra;
405	  hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). (The single-coefficient
406	  lemma \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} provides the same denominator-clearing primitive
407	  at the level of one coefficient polynomial; the proof discharges a whole generator
408	  at once using this integrality-descent step, folding that primitive over the coefficients
409	  internally.)
410	
411	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
412	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending \(X_j \mapsto b_j\),
413	  where \(b_j \in B_g\) is the image of \(b_j \in B\). We establish injectivity and
414	  module-finiteness through a comparison with the situation over \(K\).
415	
416	  \emph{Step 3a — the comparison map \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\).} Since \(g \ne 0\) in the
417	  domain \(A\) and \(A \subseteq K\), the image of \(g\) is a unit of the field \(K\), so
418	  it is invertible in \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\). Hence the canonical \(A\)-algebra map
419	  \(B \to B_K\), \(b \mapsto 1 \otimes b\), sends \(g\) to a unit and, by the universal
420	  property of localisation, factors through a canonical \(A\)-algebra map
421	  \[
422	    \nu : B_g = B[g^{-1}] \longrightarrow B_K, \qquad \nu(b/g^k) = (1 \otimes b)\,(1 \otimes g)^{-k}.
423	  \]
424	  The map \(\nu\) is injective. Indeed, \(B\) is a domain, and both \(B_g\) and \(B_K\) are
425	  localisations of \(B\) at multiplicative subsets of the non-zerodivisors: \(B_g\) inverts
426	  the powers of \(g\), while \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\) inverts (in particular) the images of
427	  all non-zero elements of \(A\), a multiplicative set containing the powers of \(g\). A
428	  localisation of a domain at a set of non-zerodivisors is injective into any further such
429	  localisation. Concretely, the natural map \(B \to B_K\) is injective on the domain \(B\)
430	  (its kernel is the set of \(b\) annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\), which in a
431	  domain over which \(A\) acts faithfully is \(0\)), so \(\nu(b/g^k) = 0\) forces
432	  \(1 \otimes b = 0\), hence \(b = 0\) and \(b/g^k = 0\). Under \(\nu\) the lifts
433	  \(b_j \in B_g\) of the Noether generators map to the elements \(\bar b_j \in B_K\) of
434	  Step 1: \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\).
435	
436	  \emph{Step 3b — injectivity of \(\varphi\) by algebraic-independence descent
437	  \(K \to A_g\).} Injectivity of the \(A_g\)-algebra map
438	  \(\varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\), \(X_j \mapsto b_j\), is equivalent to the
439	  algebraic independence of the family \((b_j)\) over \(A_g\): a non-zero element of the
440	  kernel is exactly a non-trivial \(A_g\)-polynomial relation \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\).
441	  The localisation \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\) embeds in the fraction field, giving an
442	  injection of \(A_g\)-algebras \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\). Now suppose
443	  \(P \in A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is non-zero with \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\) in \(B_g\).
444	  Applying the injective map \(\nu\) and using \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\) together with the fact
445	  that \(\nu\) is an \(A\)-algebra map extending scalars to \(K\), we obtain
446	  \(P^K(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n) = 0\) in \(B_K\), where \(P^K\) is the image of \(P\)
447	  under the coefficient inclusion \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
448	  Since \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\) is injective, \(P \ne 0\) gives \(P^K \ne 0\); but the
449	  \(\bar b_j\) are algebraically independent over \(K\) (Step 1), so the only \(K\)-polynomial
450	  vanishing on them is \(0\), a contradiction. Hence no non-trivial relation exists, \((b_j)\)
451	  is algebraically independent over \(A_g\), and \(\varphi\) is injective.
452	
453	  \emph{Step 3c — module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\).} By Step 1,
454	  \(B_K\) is module-finite over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); fix a finite set of algebra
455	  generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\) (Step 2). Each such generator
456	  satisfies a monic equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are polynomials in
457	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). The element \(g\) of Step 2 was chosen as a common
458	  denominator for the (finitely many) coefficient polynomials of these equations, so that
459	  after inverting \(g\) every such coefficient already lies in
460	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n] = \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Each chosen generator is the
461	  \(\nu\)-image of an element of \(B_g\), and its integral-dependence equation is the
462	  \(\nu\)-image of the corresponding monic equation holding in \(B_g\) with coefficients in
463	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\); since \(\nu\) is injective, that equation holds in \(B_g\)
464	  itself. Thus each generator of \(B_g\) is integral over the subalgebra
465	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\), and these generators generate \(B_g\) as an
466	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\)-algebra. A finite-type algebra that is integral over a
467	  subalgebra is module-finite over it; hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
468	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi = A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Transported along the injection
469	  \(\varphi\), this is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) for
470	  the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\), as claimed. This recovers the
471	  source's conclusion that \(B_g\) is finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
472	\end{proof}
473	
474	\begin{lemma}\leanok
475	[injectivity of a localisation lift]
476	  \label{lem:gf_isLocalization_lift_injective}
477	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalization_lift_injective}
478	  Let \(S\) be a localisation of a ring \(R\) at a multiplicative set \(M\), and let
479	  \(g : R \to P\) be a ring homomorphism sending every element of \(M\) to a unit of
480	  \(P\), so that it induces the lift \(\widehat g : S \to P\). If both the localisation
481	  map \(R \to S\) and \(g\) itself are injective, then \(\widehat g\) is injective.
482	\end{lemma}
483	\begin{proof}
484	  The lift is characterised by \(\widehat g(r/m) = g(r)\,g(m)^{-1}\). By the universal
485	  criterion for injectivity of a localisation lift, \(\widehat g\) is injective iff for
486	  all \(r_1, r_2 \in R\) the equality of localisation images
487	  \((r_1 \mapsto S) = (r_2 \mapsto S)\) implies \(g(r_1) = g(r_2)\). Both the hypothesis
488	  and the conclusion of that implication reduce, via injectivity of \(R \to S\) and of
489	  \(g\), to the single condition \(r_1 = r_2\); hence the implication holds and
490	  \(\widehat g\) is injective. (This helper is applied twice in
491	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}, to the comparison maps \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\)
492	  and \(\psi : A_g \to K\).)
493	\end{proof}
494	
495	\begin{lemma}
496	
497	
498	\leanok
499	[L5a — the generic-rank short exact sequence]
500	  \label{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses}
501	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_generic_rank_ses}
502	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
503	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1765).
504	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
505	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
506	  %  domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
507	  %  sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
508	  %  $$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
509	  %  where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
510	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (generic-rank SES).}
511	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
512	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
513	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
514	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
515	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
516	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
517	  \[
518	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
519	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
520	  \]
521	
522	  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
523	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
524	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
525	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
526	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
527	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.
528	\end{lemma}
529	
530	\begin{proof}
531	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
532	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
533	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
534	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
535	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
536	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
537	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
538	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
539	
540	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
541	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
542	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
543	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
544	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
545	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
546	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
547	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
548	
549	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
550	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
551	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
552	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
553	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
554	\end{proof}
555	
556	\begin{lemma}[L5b anchor — a finite torsion module has a non-zero-divisor annihilator]
557	  \label{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
558	  \lean{Submodule.annihilator_top_inter_nonZeroDivisors}
559	  \mathlibok
560	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
561	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727)
562	  % -- the same torsion-annihilation primitive, here provided in general by Mathlib.
563	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
564	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
565	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
566	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
567	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
568	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and \(M\) a finite torsion \(R\)-module. Then the
569	  annihilator of \(M\) meets the non-zero-divisors of \(R\): the set
570	  \((\top : \operatorname{Submodule} R\,M).\mathrm{annihilator} \cap R^{\circ}\) is
571	  non-empty, where \(R^{\circ}\) denotes the non-zero-divisors of \(R\).
572	\end{lemma}
573	
574	\begin{lemma}\leanok
575	[L5b.1 — annihilator extraction for the torsion module]
576	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator}
577	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_annihilator}
578	  \uses{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
579	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
580	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727).
581	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
582	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
583	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
584	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
585	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (torsion annihilation).}
586	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
587	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\). Then there exists a non-zero
588	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\); consequently the \(P_d\)-action on \(T\) factors
589	  through \(P_d/(F)\) and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
590	\end{lemma}
591	
592	\begin{proof}
593	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1724-L1727).
594	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (torsion annihilation).}
595	  The ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is a noetherian domain (a polynomial ring over the
596	  noetherian domain \(A\)), and \(T\) is a finite torsion \(P_d\)-module. By
597	  \cref{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors} the annihilator of \(T\) meets the
598	  non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\); choose \(F\) in that intersection. In the domain \(P_d\) a
599	  non-zero-divisor is exactly a non-zero element, so \(F \ne 0\), and \(F \cdot T = 0\).
600	  Since \(F\) annihilates \(T\), the \(P_d\)-module structure descends along the quotient
601	  map \(P_d \twoheadrightarrow P_d/(F)\); the image of any finite \(P_d\)-generating set of
602	  \(T\) generates \(T\) over \(P_d/(F)\), so \(T\) is finite over \(P_d/(F)\).
603	\end{proof}

605	\subsubsection{Nagata change-of-variables machinery}
606	\label{sec:gf_nagata_machinery}
607	
608	The Nagata normalisation lemma \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} is assembled from
609	technical helper lemmas implementing the triangular change of variables and the
610	radix-encoding degree bookkeeping, following the Nagata trick of
611	\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 over a noetherian domain. Throughout, \(k\) is a domain,
612	\(n \ge 0\), and \(f \in k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is a fixed polynomial; we write
613	\(\mathrm{up} := 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}}(f)\) and the radix weights \(r_i := \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\)
614	for \(i = 0, \dots, n\) (so every exponent occurring in \(f\) is a base-\(\mathrm{up}\) digit).
615	The distinguished variable is \(X_0\).
616	
617	\begin{definition}\leanok
618	[Nagata substitution \(T_1\)]
619	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T1}
620	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T1}
621	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
622	  For a scalar \(c \in k\), the substitution \(T_1(c)\) is the \(k\)-algebra endomorphism
623	  of \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) determined on generators by
624	  \[
625	    X_0 \mapsto X_0, \qquad X_i \mapsto X_i + c\,X_0^{\,r_i} \quad (i \ne 0).
626	  \]
627	  It is triangular in the variables \(X_i\) (\(i \ne 0\)) over the distinguished variable
628	  \(X_0\); this is the device realising the support-dimension drop.
629	\end{definition}
630	
631	\begin{lemma}\leanok
632	[\(T_1\) is invertible by the opposite shift]
633	  \label{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
634	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.t1_comp_t1_neg}
635	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
636	  For every \(c \in k\), \(T_1(c) \circ T_1(-c) = \mathrm{id}\); the Nagata substitution is
637	  invertible, with inverse the substitution of opposite coefficient.
638	\end{lemma}
639	\begin{proof}
640	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
641	  It suffices to check the two composites agree on each generator: \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), and
642	  \(X_i \mapsto X_i + (-c)X_0^{r_i} + c\,X_0^{r_i} = X_i\) for \(i \ne 0\).
643	\end{proof}
644	
645	\begin{definition}\leanok
646	[Nagata automorphism \(T\)]
647	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T}
648	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T}
649	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1, lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
650	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
651	  The Nagata change of variables is the \(k\)-algebra automorphism \(T := T_1(1)\) of
652	  \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) — i.e.\ \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{\,r_i}\) (\(i \ne 0\)) and
653	  \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\) — packaged as an algebra equivalence with inverse \(T_1(-1)\) via
654	  \cref{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}.
655	\end{definition}
656	
657	\begin{lemma}\leanok
658	[Exponent bound is preserved by listing]
659	  \label{lem:gf_lt_up}
660	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.lt_up}
661	  If every entry of an exponent vector \(v\) satisfies \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\), then
662	  every entry of the list of values of \(v\) is also \(< \mathrm{up}\).
663	\end{lemma}
664	\begin{proof}
665	  The list of values of \(v\) consists exactly of the entries \(v_i\); each satisfies
666	  \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\) by hypothesis.
667	\end{proof}
668	
669	\begin{lemma}\leanok
670	[Injectivity of the radix encoding]
671	  \label{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
672	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.sum_r_mul_ne}
673	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
674	  Let \(v, w\) be exponent vectors with all entries \(< \mathrm{up}\) and \(v \ne w\). Then
675	  the weighted base-\(\mathrm{up}\) sums differ:
676	  \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\), where \(r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\).
677	\end{lemma}
678	\begin{proof}
679	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
680	  The map \(v \mapsto \sum_i \mathrm{up}^{\,i} v_i\) is the value of the base-\(\mathrm{up}\)
681	  digit string \(v\); since every digit is \(< \mathrm{up}\) (\cref{lem:gf_lt_up}), this
682	  encoding is injective by uniqueness of base-\(\mathrm{up}\) expansions, so distinct
683	  \(v \ne w\) give distinct sums.
684	\end{proof}
685	
686	\begin{lemma}\leanok
687	[\(X_0\)-degree of a transformed monomial]
688	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t}
689	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_zero_t}
690	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
691	  For a non-zero scalar \(a \ne 0\) and exponent vector \(v\), the degree in \(X_0\) of the
692	  transformed monomial is the radix value
693	  \[
694	    \deg_{X_0}\!\big(T(a\,X^v)\big) = \sum_i r_i v_i, \qquad r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}.
695	  \]
696	\end{lemma}
697	\begin{proof}
698	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
699	  Under \(T\), \(a\,X^v \mapsto a\,X_0^{v_0}\prod_{i \ne 0}(X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\). Read as
700	  a polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), each factor
701	  \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) has \(X_0\)-degree \(r_i v_i\) and leading coefficient \(1\);
702	  the degrees add to \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) (the hypothesis \(a \ne 0\) keeps the top term from
703	  vanishing over the domain \(k\)).
704	\end{proof}
705	
706	\begin{lemma}\leanok
707	[Distinct monomials acquire distinct \(X_0\)-degrees]
708	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
709	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
710	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
711	  For distinct support exponents \(v \ne w\) of \(f\), the transformed monomials
712	  \(T(c_v X^v)\) and \(T(c_w X^w)\) (where \(c_v, c_w\) are the corresponding coefficients
713	  of \(f\)) have distinct degrees in \(X_0\), making the top \(X_0\)-degree of \(T(f)\) unique.
714	\end{lemma}
715	\begin{proof}
716	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
717	  By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t} the two degrees are \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) and
718	  \(\sum_i r_i w_i\). Each support exponent is bounded by the total degree of \(f\), hence
719	  \(< \mathrm{up} = 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}} f\); radix-injectivity
720	  \cref{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne} then yields \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\).
721	\end{proof}
722	
723	\begin{lemma}\leanok
724	[Leading coefficient of a transformed monomial]
725	  \label{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
726	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
727	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
728	  For any exponent \(v\) with coefficient \(c_v := [X^v]\,f\), viewing \(T(c_v X^v)\) as a
729	  univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), its leading coefficient in
730	  \(X_0\) is the constant \(c_v \in k\).
731	\end{lemma}
732	\begin{proof}
733	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
734	  Each factor \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) is monic in \(X_0\) with leading coefficient
735	  \(1\); a product of monics is monic, so the leading coefficient of \(T(c_v X^v)\) in
736	  \(X_0\) is exactly the scalar \(c_v\).
737	\end{proof}
738	
739	\begin{lemma}\leanok
740	[Leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) is a non-zero constant]
741	  \label{lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq}
742	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T_leadingcoeff_eq}
743	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
744	  Suppose \(f \ne 0\). Then there is a support exponent \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\) such that
745	  the leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) in \(X_0\) equals the constant \(c_v := [X^v]\,f \ne 0\).
746	  Thus \(T(f)\) is, up to the non-zero scalar \(c_v\), monic in \(X_0\).
747	\end{lemma}
748	\begin{proof}
749	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
750	  Expand \(f = \sum_{w \in \mathrm{supp}\,f} c_w X^w\). By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
751	  the transformed monomials \(T(c_w X^w)\) have pairwise distinct \(X_0\)-degrees, so one of
752	  them — say at \(v\) — strictly dominates the others; the leading term of
753	  \(T(f) = \sum_w T(c_w X^w)\) is then that of \(T(c_v X^v)\), whose leading coefficient is
754	  the constant \(c_v\) by \cref{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}. Since
755	  \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\), \(c_v \ne 0\).
756	\end{proof}
757	
758	\begin{lemma}\leanok
759	[Naturality of the variable-splitting isomorphism under base change]
760	  \label{lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
761	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
762	  For any ring homomorphism \(\varphi : A \to B\) and polynomial \(q \in A[X_0, \dots, X_m]\),

799	  Let \(A\) be a domain, \(n \ge 0\), and \(0 \ne F \in A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\). Then there
800	  exist a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) and a

803	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), has a \emph{unit} leading coefficient. Equivalently, \(e(F)\)

806	
807	\begin{proof}
808	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
809	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop, Nagata normalisation).}
810	  Apply the Nagata substitution \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{r^{\,i}}\) for \(i = 1, \dots, n\)
811	  and \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), with \(r\) larger than every exponent occurring in \(F\): it is a
812	  triangular (hence invertible) \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\).
813	  Under this substitution the distinct monomials of \(F\) acquire distinct degrees in
814	  \(X_0\), so \(e(F)\), expanded as a polynomial in \(X_0\), has a unique top-degree term
815	  whose coefficient is the coefficient \(c \in A\) of the top total-degree monomial of
816	  \(F\); since \(F \ne 0\) this \(c \ne 0\). Put \(g := c\). The degree of \(e(F)\) in
817	  \(X_0\) equals the total degree of \(F\), and its leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is the
818	  constant \(g\). Passing to \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\), the image of \(g\) is a unit, so the
819	  image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\).
820	\end{proof}

979	\subsubsection{Module-structure transport helpers (reindex)}
980	\label{sec:gf_reindex_transport_helpers}
981	
982	The localisation-transport step of \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} moves a module structure
983	and its finiteness across (i) an additive equivalence of the underlying groups, (ii) a ring
984	isomorphism of the acting ring, and (iii) a change of localising monoid along a scalar
985	tower. The following helper lemmas package those three transport principles. They are general
986	module-theory facts; no external source is cited.
987	
988	\begin{lemma}\leanok
989	[Module transport along an additive equivalence]
990	  \label{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}
991	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
992	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
993	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullback_isScalarTower}
994	  Let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} N\) be an additive equivalence and let \(M\) carry an
995	  \(R\)-module structure. Pulling the action back along \(e\) by
996	  \(r \cdot y := e\big(r \cdot e^{-1}(y)\big)\) makes \(N\) an \(R\)-module for which \(e\)
997	  is \(R\)-linear. Under this transported structure: (a) if \(M\) is module-finite over
998	  \(R\) then so is \(N\); and (b) if \(M\) additionally carries a compatible action of a
999	  base ring \(A_g\) with scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to M\), then the pulled-back actions on
1000	  \(N\) again form a scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to N\).
1001	\end{lemma}
1002	\begin{proof}
1003	  The module axioms for \(y \mapsto e(r \cdot e^{-1}(y))\) follow termwise from those on
1004	  \(M\) together with the fact that \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\) are additive and mutually inverse;
1005	  by construction \(e\) is then an \(R\)-linear isomorphism. Statement (a) follows by
1006	  transporting module-finiteness across the \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\).
1007	  Statement (b) checks the scalar-tower identity
1008	  \((a\,r)\cdot y = a\cdot(r\cdot y)\) on \(N\) by transporting it across \(e\) and applying
1009	  associativity of the scalar action on \(M\).
1010	\end{proof}

1075	  \label{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1076	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_away_tower}
1077	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1078	  Let \(A\) be a domain and \(T\) an \(A\)-module. Let \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), and let
1079	  \(h \in A_g := \mathrm{Away}(g)\) be non-zero. Write
1080	  \((T_g)_h\) for the iterated localisation of \(T\) — first away from \(g\), then away from
1081	  \(h\) — and \((A_g)_h := \mathrm{Away}(h)\) for the corresponding iterated localisation of the
1082	  base ring. If \((T_g)_h\) is free over \((A_g)_h\), then there exists \(f \in A\),
1083	  \(f \ne 0\), such that the single localisation \(T_f\) is free over \(A_f := \mathrm{Away}(f)\).
1084	  Concretely the witness is the single product \(f := g\,a\) for an element \(a \in A\) whose
1085	  image in \(A_g\) is associated to \(h\).
1086	\end{lemma}
1087	
1088	\begin{proof}
1089	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1090	  \emph{Clear the denominator of \(h\).} As \(h\) lies in \(A_g = \mathrm{Away}(g)\),
1091	  surjectivity of the localisation map \(A \to A_g\) onto fractions yields \(a \in A\) and a
1092	  power \(s\) of \(g\) with \(h \cdot \bar s = \bar a\) in \(A_g\), where \(\bar{\,\cdot\,}\)
1093	  denotes the image in \(A_g\). The image \(\bar s\) is a unit (a power of \(g\) is invertible
1094	  in \(A_g\)), so \(\bar a = h\,\bar s\) is associated to \(h\); in particular \(\bar a \ne 0\)
1095	  (because \(h \ne 0\)), hence \(a \ne 0\). Since \(A\) is a domain and \(g, a \ne 0\), the
1096	  product \(f := g\,a\) is non-zero.
1097	
1098	  \emph{Identify the base rings.} The associatedness of \(\bar a\) and \(h\), together with the
1099	  tower \(A \to A_g \to A_h\), identifies the iterated localisation
1100	  \((A_g)_h = \mathrm{Away}(h)\) with the single localisation of \(A\) away from the product
1101	  \(g\,a\): by \cref{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated} (with \(x = g\), \(z = a\),
1102	  \(y = h\)) the ring \(A_h\) is a localisation of \(A\) away from \(g\,a\), and uniqueness of
1103	  localisation furnishes a ring isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \xrightarrow{\sim} A_h\).
1104	
1105	  \emph{Identify the modules.} The composite localisation map
1106	  \[
1107	    \psi : T \longrightarrow (T_g)_h, \qquad
1108	    \psi := \mathrm{mk}_{\langle h\rangle} \circ \mathrm{mk}_{\langle g\rangle},
1109	  \]
1110	  first into \(T_g\) and then into \((T_g)_h\), realises \((T_g)_h\) as the localisation of
1111	  \(T\) at the powers of \(g\,a\): every \(g^n a^m\) acts invertibly on \((T_g)_h\), \(\psi\) is
1112	  surjective up to such denominators, and its kernel is the expected one. This
1113	  localisation-of-localisation identity is verified directly from the defining properties of a
1114	  localised module; no packaged lemma supplies it.
1115	
1116	  \emph{Transport freeness.} By hypothesis \((T_g)_h\) is free over \(A_h\). The ring
1117	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \cong A_h\) and the induced semilinear module equivalence
1118	  \(T_{g\,a} \simeq (T_g)_h\) — assembled from the localised-module identification of the
1119	  previous step and promoted to an \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\)-linear equivalence — carry this
1120	  freeness back by \cref{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}: \(T_{g\,a}\) is free over
1121	  \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\). Taking \(f := g\,a\) gives \(T_f\) free over \(A_f\), as required.
1122	\end{proof}
1123	
1124	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1125	[L5 — polynomial-ring core of generic freeness]
1126	  \label{lem:gf_polynomial_core}
1127	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_polynomial}
1128	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1129	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1130	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1131	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
1132	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1772).
1133	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1134	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the domain
1135	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact sequence of
1136	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
1137	  %  $0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$ where $T$ is a finite
1138	  %  torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Therefore, the dimension of the
1139	  %  support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$ as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less
1140	  %  than $n$. Hence by induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),
1141	  %  there exists some $h\ne 0$ in $A$ with $T_h$ free over $A_{gh}$. Taking $f=gh$,
1142	  %  the lemma follows from the above short exact sequence."
1143	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (polynomial-ring
1144	  core of the induction).}
1145	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(d \ge 0\). Let \(N\) be a finite module over
1146	  the polynomial ring \(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\), regarded as an \(A\)-module. Then there
1147	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1148	
1149	\end{lemma}
1150	
1151	\begin{proof}
1152	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1153	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1154	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1155	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1772).
1156	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
1157	  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
1158	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
1159	  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
1160	  corresponding polynomial ring.
1161	
1162	  \emph{Base case \(d = 0\).} The ring \(P_0 = A[X_1, \dots, X_0]\) is \(A\) itself, so
1163	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
1164	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.
1165	
1166	  \emph{Inductive step \(d \ge 1\).} If \(N\) is already a torsion \(P_d\)-module over
1167	  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
1168	  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
1169	  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.
1170	  \begin{enumerate}
1171	    \item \textbf{Generic-rank SES.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses} to \(N\) over
1172	      \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\): there are \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective
1173	      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
1174	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
1175	      \(P_d\)-modules
1176	      \[
1177	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
1178	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
1179	      \]
1180	      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
1181	    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
1182	      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
1183	      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1184	      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
1185	    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
1186	      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base
1187	      \(A_g\)} to the finite \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module \(T_g\): there is
1188	      \(h \ne 0\) in \(A_g\) with \((T_g)_h\) free over \((A_g)_h\). This is exactly the
1189	      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)
1190	      to \(A_g\).
1191	    \item \textbf{Descend the witness from \(A_g\) to \(A\).} Clearing the denominator of
1192	      \(h\) over \(A_g\) produces a numerator \(a \in A\) whose image in \(A_g\) is associated
1193	      to \(h\); then the iterated localisation \((A_g)_h\) is the single localisation
1194	      \(A_{g a}\) and \((T_g)_h\) is \(T_{g a}\). Thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over
1195	      \((A_g)_h\) transports to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the
1196	      iterated-\(\mathrm{Away}\)-tower descent \cref{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}. Set
1197	      \(f := g a \ne 0\) — the single product, \emph{not} a square — so that \(T_f\) is free
1198	      over \(A_f\).
1199	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
1200	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
1201	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
1202	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
1203	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
1204	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
1205	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
1206	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
1207	      completes the induction.
1208	  \end{enumerate}
1209	\end{proof}

1255	\subsection{Geometric bridges for the affine assembly}
1256	\label{sec:gf_geometric_bridges}
1257	
1258	The geometric form of generic flatness is assembled from the algebraic core by
1259	restricting to a non-empty affine open of \(S\) and working patchwise over a finite
1260	affine cover of the preimage. Two genuinely geometric steps are isolated here as
1261	named bridges: the affine-local finiteness of the section module of a finite-type
1262	quasi-coherent sheaf (G1), feeding the algebraic core its finite-module input; and
1263	the flat-locality assembly (G3), promoting per-patch freeness to flatness of the
1264	restricted sheaf over the base open.
1265	
1266	\begin{lemma}[Finiteness is local on a finite spanning family]
1267	  \label{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan}
1268	  \lean{Module.Finite.of_localizationSpan_finite}
1269	  \mathlibok
1270	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.LocalProperties.Submodule}).}
1271	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(N\) an \(R\)-module, and \(t \subseteq R\) a
1272	  finite family with \(\mathrm{span}\,t = \top\) (the \(t\) generate the unit
1273	  ideal). Suppose that for each \(g \in t\) the localization \(N_g\) is a finite
1274	  module over the localization \(R_g\). Then \(N\) is a finite \(R\)-module.
1275	\end{lemma}
1276	
1277	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1278	[Half 1 helper — transfer of localized-module finiteness across models]
1279	  \label{lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer}
1280	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.finite_localizedModule_of_isLocalizedModule}
1281	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative submonoid,
1282	  and \(M\) an \(R\)-module. Let \(R_S\) be an \(R\)-algebra that localizes \(R\)
1283	  at \(S\), and let \(N\) be an \(R\)-module carrying a compatible \(R_S\)-module
1284	  structure, together with an \(R\)-linear map \(\varphi : M \to N\) exhibiting
1285	  \(N\) as the localization of \(M\) at \(S\) (\(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\,S\,
1286	  \varphi\)). If \(N\) is a finite \(R_S\)-module, then the canonical localized
1287	  module \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) is a finite module over the canonical
1288	  localization \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
1289	\end{lemma}
1290	
1291	\begin{proof}
1292	  The localization map \(\varphi\) provides an \(R\)-linear isomorphism
1293	  \(e : N \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) (universal property
1294	  of localized modules), and the algebra localization provides an \(R\)-algebra
1295	  isomorphism \(\psi : R_S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{Localization}\,S\). These
1296	  are compatible: \(e\) is semilinear over \(\psi\), i.e.\ \(e(a \cdot x) =
1297	  \psi(a) \cdot e(x)\) for \(a \in R_S\), \(x \in N\) (both sides are pinned by
1298	  clearing a common denominator \(s \in S\), where the \(R\)-actions agree). A
1299	  finite \(R_S\)-spanning set \(T\) of \(N\) therefore maps under \(e\) to a
1300	  finite set whose \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\)-span is all of
1301	  \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\); hence the canonical model is finite over
1302	  \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
1303	\end{proof}

1305	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1306	[Half 1 — G1 locality reduction over a finite basic-open cover]

1320	  family with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) (so the basic opens \(D(g)\),
1321	  \(g \in t\), cover \(W\)), and that for each \(g \in t\) the section module
1322	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\). Then \(\Gamma(\F, W)\)

1326	\begin{proof}
1327	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen, lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer,

1329	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (gluing step).}
1330	  By the local-on-a-spanning-family criterion \cref{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan}
1331	  it suffices to show, for each \(g \in t\), that the localization of
1332	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) at \(g\) is finite over the localization
1333	  \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\). Fix \(g \in t\). The basic open \(D(g)\) is affine, and the
1334	  restriction \(\Gamma(X, W) \to \Gamma(X, D(g))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\) as
1335	  the away-localization \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\)
1336	  (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); moreover the section restriction
1337	  \(\Gamma(\F, W) \to \Gamma(\F, D(g))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) as the
1338	  localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W)\)
1339	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}). Thus \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is a

1349	\subsection{Transport engine for generating sections}
1350	
1351	The restriction-of-generators lemma below is assembled from a generic transport
1352	principle: a finite generating family of a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{M}\) pushes
1353	forward to a finite generating family of \(F(\mathcal{M})\) along any functor \(F\) of
1354	sheaves of modules that preserves colimits and carries the structure sheaf to the
1355	structure sheaf. The three blocks of this subsection package that principle (the
1356	generating family, its index type, and the finiteness bookkeeping). They are
1357	project-bespoke: Mathlib provides the analogous transport only for full presentations
1358	(with kernel data), not for bare generating families.
1359	
1360	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1361	[Transport of a generating family along a colimit-preserving functor]
1362	  \label{lem:genSections_map}
1363	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.GeneratingSections.map}
1364	  Let \(F : \mathrm{Mod}\,R \to \mathrm{Mod}\,S\) be a functor of sheaves of modules
1365	  that preserves colimits and is equipped with an isomorphism
1366	  \(\eta : F(\OO_R) \xrightarrow{\sim} \OO_S\) carrying the unit (structure sheaf) of the
1367	  source to that of the target. Given a generating family \(\sigma\) of \(\mathcal{M}\)
1368	  with index set \(I = \sigma.I\) and epimorphism
1369	  \(\sigma.\pi : \OO_R^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{M}\), the images
1370	  \((\mathrm{mapFree}\,F\,\eta\,I)^{-1} \circ F(\sigma.\pi)\) form a generating family of
1371	  \(F(\mathcal{M})\) with the same index set \(I\). The map is an epimorphism because it
1372	  is the composite of an isomorphism with \(F(\sigma.\pi)\), and \(F\) preserves the
1373	  epimorphism \(\sigma.\pi\).
1374	\end{lemma}
1375	
1376	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1377	[The transported family keeps its index type]
1378	  \label{lem:genSections_map_I}
1379	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.GeneratingSections.map_I}
1380	  \uses{lem:genSections_map}
1381	  The index set of the transported generating family of \cref{lem:genSections_map} equals
1382	  the index set of the original: \((\mathrm{map}\,\sigma\,F\,\eta)\!.I = \sigma.I\). This
1383	  holds definitionally.
1384	\end{lemma}
1385	
1386	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1387	[Finiteness survives transport]
1388	  \label{lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}
1389	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.GeneratingSections.map_isFiniteType}
1390	  \uses{lem:genSections_map, lem:genSections_map_I}
1391	  If the original generating family \(\sigma\) is of finite type (its index set is finite),
1392	  then so is the transported family \(\mathrm{map}\,\sigma\,F\,\eta\): the index type is
1393	  unchanged by \cref{lem:genSections_map_I}, hence still finite.
1394	\end{lemma}
1395	
1396	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1397	[Half 2, seam 1a — local generators restrict to a smaller open]
1398	  \label{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict}
1399	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_localGenerators_restrict}
1400	  \uses{lem:genSections_map, lem:genSections_map_I, lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}
1401	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
1402	  Suppose that on an open \(Y \subseteq X\) the restriction \(\F|_Y\) is
1403	  globally generated by finitely many sections. Then for any open \(V \le Y\) the
1404	  restricted family — the images of those generators under the section-restriction
1405	  \(\Gamma(\F, Y) \to \Gamma(\F, V)\) — is again a finite generating family of
1406	  \(\F|_V\). Generation survives passage to the smaller open (a family that generates
1407	  every stalk over \(Y\) generates every stalk over \(V\)), and the index set, and hence
1408	  finiteness, is unchanged.
1409	\end{lemma}
1410	
1411	\begin{proof}
1412	  \uses{lem:genSections_map, lem:genSections_map_I, lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}
1413	  A family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) generates \(\F|_Y\) precisely when the
1414	  induced map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) is surjective on every stalk
1415	  \(\F_y\,(y \in Y)\). Restricting along the inclusion \(V \le Y\) the
1416	  generators map to \(\{s_i|_V\}_{i \in I}\), and for a point \(x \in V\) the stalk
1417	  \(\F_x\) and the comparison map agree with the value at \(x\) computed over \(Y\), since
1418	  stalks are insensitive to shrinking the ambient open. Hence the induced map
1419	  \(\OO_V^{\oplus I} \to \F|_V\) is surjective on every stalk over \(V\), so
1420	  \(\{s_i|_V\}_{i \in I}\) generates \(\F|_V\). The index set \(I\) is the same
1421	  finite set, so finiteness is preserved. Concretely the restricted family is obtained by
1422	  transporting the generating family along the (colimit-preserving, unit-preserving)
1423	  restriction functors via the transport engine \cref{lem:genSections_map}, whose index
1424	  type is unchanged (\cref{lem:genSections_map_I}) and which preserves finiteness
1425	  (\cref{lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}).
1426	\end{proof}
1427	
1428	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1429	[Half 2, seam 1b — finite standard-basic-open subcover of an affine open]
1430	  \label{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover}
1431	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover}
1432	  Let \(W\) be an affine open of a scheme \(X\), with the canonical identification
1433	  \(W \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\). Given an arbitrary open cover \(\{U_\alpha\}\) of \(W\),
1434	  there is a finite family \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with
1435	  \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) such that each basic open \(D(g)\) (\(g \in t\)) is
1436	  contained in some cover member \(U_\alpha\). In particular the \(D(g)\,(g \in t)\) form a
1437	  finite standard-basic-open subcover of \(W\) refining \(\{U_\alpha\}\).
1438	\end{lemma}
1439	
1440	\begin{proof}
1441	  Under \(W \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) the basic opens \(D(g)\,(g \in \Gamma(X, W))\) form a
1442	  basis of the topology (\(\mathrm{PrimeSpectrum.isBasis\_basic\_opens}\)). Hence each
1443	  cover member \(U_\alpha\) is a union of basic opens, and the collection of all basic
1444	  opens \(D(g)\) that are contained in some \(U_\alpha\) still covers \(W\). The affine
1445	  scheme \(W\) is quasi-compact, so finitely many of these basic opens
1446	  \(D(g_1), \dots, D(g_n)\) already cover \(W\); set
1447	  \(t = \{g_1, \dots, g_n\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\), a finite family, each \(D(g_i)\)
1448	  contained in some \(U_\alpha\) by construction. That the \(D(g_i)\) cover
1449	  \(W = \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) — i.e. no prime ideal contains all the \(g_i\) — is exactly
1450	  the condition \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\).
1451	\end{proof}
1452	
1453	\begin{lemma}[Half 2, seam 1c — finite generation equals a finite free epimorphism]
1454	  \label{lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1455	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1456	  Let \(Y\) be an open of a scheme and \(\F\) a sheaf of modules on \(Y\). Then
1457	  \(\F\) is globally generated by a finite family of sections if and only if there is a
1458	  finite index set \(I\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_Y\)-modules
1459	  \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F\). No quasi-coherence of \(\F\) is assumed:
1460	  the statement holds for arbitrary sheaves of modules on \(Y\), and applies in particular
1461	  to the restrictions \(\F|_{D(g)}\) arising in the cover assembly.
1462	\end{lemma}
1463	
1464	\begin{proof}
1465	  A finite family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) of \(\F|_Y\) is the same datum as an
1466	  \(\OO_Y\)-linear map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) sending the \(i\)-th basis section to
1467	  \(s_i\). The family generates \(\F|_Y\) (the induced map is surjective on every stalk)
1468	  exactly when this map is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules. Thus a finite generating
1469	  family yields a finite-free epimorphism, and conversely the images of the basis sections
1470	  of any epimorphism \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_Y\) with \(I\) finite form a
1471	  finite generating family.
1472	\end{proof}
1473	
1474	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1475	[Half 2, seam 1 — finite type yields a finite cover of globally-generated affines]
1476	  \label{lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated}
1477	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated}
1478	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof
1479	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2101-L2107).
1480	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1481	  % "Assume $\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. This means
1482	  %  there exists an open covering of $X$ such that $\widetilde M$ restricted to
1483	  %  the members of this covering is globally generated by finitely many sections.
1484	  %  Thus there also exists a standard open covering
1485	  %  $X = \bigcup_{i = 1, \ldots, n} D(f_i)$ such that $\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$
1486	  %  is generated by finitely many sections."
1487	  \uses{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict, lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover,
1488	        lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1489	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (standard
1490	  cover of generated affines).}
1491	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\F\) a sheaf of modules of finite type,
1492	  and \(W \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then there is a finite family
1493	  \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) such that for
1494	  each \(g \in t\) the basic open \(D(g)\) is affine and \(\F|_{D(g)}\) is globally
1495	  generated by finitely many sections; equivalently, for each \(g \in t\) there is
1496	  a finite index set \(I_g\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_{D(g)}\)-modules
1497	  \(\OO_{D(g)}^{\oplus I_g} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_{D(g)}\).
1498	\end{lemma}
1499	
1500	\begin{proof}
1501	  \uses{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict, lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover,
1502	        lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1503	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (standard cover of generated affines).}
1504	  Finite type of \(\F\) means that \(X\) has an open covering \(\{Y_\alpha\}\) on each
1505	  member of which \(\F\) carries a finite generating family of sections. The intersections
1506	  \(\{Y_\alpha \cap W\}\) form an open cover of the affine \(W\). By the finite standard
1507	  subcover refinement \cref{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover} there is a finite family
1508	  \(t = \{g_1, \dots, g_n\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with
1509	  \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) and each basic open \(D(g_i)\) contained in some
1510	  \(Y_\alpha \cap W\) — in particular \(D(g_i) \subseteq W\) is affine, and \(D(g_i)\)
1511	  embeds by an open immersion into the member \(Y_\alpha\) on which \(\F\) is finitely
1512	  generated. By the restriction-of-generators lemma
1513	  \cref{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict} the finite generating family on \(Y_\alpha\)
1514	  restricts to a finite generating family of \(\F|_{D(g_i)}\), so \(\F|_{D(g_i)}\) is
1515	  globally generated by finitely many sections. Finally, by the free-epimorphism
1516	  reformulation \cref{lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}, this is equivalent to the existence
1517	  of a finite index set \(I_{g_i}\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_{D(g_i)}\)-modules
1518	  \(\OO_{D(g_i)}^{\oplus I_{g_i}} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_{D(g_i)}\). That the \(D(g_i)\)
1519	  cover \(W = \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) is exactly \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\). This is
1520	  Stacks' passage from the local-generation datum to a standard finite cover of generated
1521	  affines.
1522	\end{proof}

1524	\begin{lemma}[Affine $\widetilde{(-)}\dashv\Gamma$ adjunction with its $\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}$ counit]
1525	  \label{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib}
1526	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tilde.adjunction}
1527	  \mathlibok
1528	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde}).}
1529	  On the affine scheme \(\Spec R\) the associated-sheaf functor
1530	  \(\widetilde{(-)} : \mathrm{Mod}\,R \to (\Spec R).\mathrm{Modules}\) is left adjoint to the
1531	  affine section functor \(\Gamma : (\Spec R).\mathrm{Modules} \to \mathrm{Mod}\,R\) (Mathlib's
1532	  \(\mathrm{moduleSpecΓFunctor}\), evaluation of a sheaf of modules at the top open). The counit of
1533	  this adjunction is the natural transformation
1534	  \(\Gamma(-)\,\widetilde{\phantom{x}} \to \mathrm{id}\) whose component at a sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\)
1535	  is \(\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ} : \widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, \top)} \to \mathcal{M}\), and
1536	  the left adjoint \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is faithful.
1537	\end{lemma}

1539	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1540	[Half 2, seam 2 — $\Gamma$ on affine quasi-coherent modules sends epis to surjections]
1541	  \label{lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi}
1542	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi}
1543	  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization, lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib,
1544	    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
1545	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof
1546	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2106-L2107) — the implication
1547	  % "$\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$ generated by finitely many sections $\Rightarrow$
1548	  % $M_{f_i}$ finitely generated" is exactly section-level surjectivity of a sheaf
1549	  % epi over an affine.
1550	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1551	  % "such that $\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$ is generated by finitely many sections.
1552	  %  Thus $M_{f_i}$ is finitely generated for each $i$."
1553	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (sections of a
1554	  generated sheaf over an affine).}
1555	  Let \(V\) be an affine open of a scheme, with \(B = \Gamma(X, V)\) and
1556	  \(V \cong \Spec B\), and let \(\pi : \mathcal{G} \to \F\) be an epimorphism of
1557	  quasi-coherent sheaves of modules over \(V\). Then the induced map on sections
1558	  \(\Gamma(\pi) : \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to \Gamma(\F, V)\) is a surjective
1559	  \(B\)-module map.
1560	\end{lemma}
1561	
1562	\begin{proof}
1563	  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization, lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib,
1564	    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
1565	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (sections of a generated sheaf over an
1566	  affine).}
1567	  Write \(V \cong \Spec B\) and let \(\Gamma\) be the affine section functor on
1568	  \((\Spec B).\mathrm{Modules}\), so that \(\Gamma(\pi) = \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to
1569	  \Gamma(\F, V)\) is the map in question. By \cref{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib} the
1570	  associated-sheaf functor \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is left adjoint to \(\Gamma\), with counit the
1571	  natural transformation whose component at a sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is
1572	  \(\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ} : \widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, V)} \to \mathcal{M}\).
1573	
1574	  Because \(\mathcal{G}\) and \(\F\) are quasi-coherent, the qcoh-sections descent
1575	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}, fed through the counit-iso assembly
1576	  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}) makes both counit components
1577	  \(\mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) and \(\F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) isomorphisms. Naturality of the
1578	  counit on \(\pi\) gives the commuting square
1579	  \[
1580	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)} \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1}
1581	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi,
1582	    \qquad\text{equivalently}\qquad
1583	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}
1584	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}.
1585	  \]
1586	  The right-hand side is a composite of an isomorphism, the epimorphism \(\pi\), and an
1587	  isomorphism, hence is itself an epimorphism. Thus \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}\) is an epimorphism
1588	  of sheaves of modules. Since the left adjoint \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is faithful, it reflects
1589	  epimorphisms; therefore \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is an epimorphism in \(\mathrm{Mod}\,B\), i.e. the
1590	  \(B\)-linear map \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is surjective.
1591	
1592	  This is the structural content of the vanishing of higher quasi-coherent cohomology on an
1593	  affine — the failure of \(\Gamma\) to be right exact would obstruct exactly this surjectivity —
1594	  obtained here directly from the affine adjunction without invoking a cohomology computation.
1595	\end{proof}
1596

1606	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module

1611	  quasi-coherent sheaves of modules over \(V\). Suppose the source has finite sections:
